title: Japanese attack on Pearl Harbor
summary: Just before eight o'clock on the morning of Sunday 7 December 1941 more than 350 aircraft launched from six Japanese aircraft carriers struck the United States Pacific Fleet at anchor in Pearl Harbor on the Hawaiian island of Oahu. In under two hours they killed roughly 2,400 Americans, sank or damaged eight battleships, and destroyed some 180 aircraft on the ground. The attack, aimed at buying Japan time to complete its conquest of Southeast Asia, instead brought the United States into the Second World War and set the stage for the eventual defeat of both Japan and Nazi Germany.

sections.heading: Background
sections.body:
The road to Pearl Harbor ran through a decade of hardening Japanese expansion in East Asia. Since seizing Manchuria in 1931 and beginning a full-scale war against China in 1937, Japan had bogged down in a conflict from which it could neither win a decisive victory nor extricate itself. In September 1940 it joined Germany and Italy in the Tripartite Pact and occupied northern French Indochina; the American response was a partial embargo on iron and steel exports. In July 1941, after Japan moved into southern Indochina, President Franklin D. Roosevelt froze Japanese assets in the United States and, in effect, cut off the oil on which the Japanese navy and much of its industry depended. Japan had perhaps eighteen months of reserves.

Inside Tokyo the debate over the following months narrowed to two courses. Diplomatically Japan sought to preserve its gains in China and Indochina while resuming imports through negotiations with Washington; strategically, if talks failed, the Imperial Japanese Navy under Admiral Isoroku Yamamoto proposed to open a war by neutralising the American Pacific Fleet in a surprise strike, then rapidly seizing the oil of the Dutch East Indies, the rubber of Malaya, and the rice of Thailand and Burma before the United States could regenerate its power. Yamamoto, who had studied at Harvard and served as naval attache in Washington, expected in his private words to 'run wild for six months or a year' but doubted Japan could win a long war against American industry.

On 16 October 1941 the more moderate cabinet of Prince Fumimaro Konoe collapsed and was replaced by that of General Hideki Tojo. On 5 November an imperial conference set a deadline: if talks in Washington had not produced a settlement by early December, Japan would go to war. Envoy Saburo Kurusu joined Ambassador Kichisaburo Nomura in a last round of talks, but Secretary of State Cordell Hull's note of 26 November, demanding Japanese withdrawal from Indochina and China, was read in Tokyo as an ultimatum. On the same day the Kido Butai, the Japanese First Air Fleet under Vice Admiral Chuichi Nagumo, sailed from Hitokappu Bay in the Kuriles on a northern route towards Hawaii. American codebreakers had partly cracked the Japanese diplomatic 'Purple' cipher but not naval codes, and the fleet's movement was concealed by radio silence. Warnings that war was imminent were sent to Pacific commanders in late November; specific warnings of an attack on Pearl Harbor were not.

sections.heading: What happened
sections.body:
At about 06:00 on 7 December 1941, from a launch point some 230 miles north of Oahu, the six Japanese carriers Akagi, Kaga, Soryu, Hiryu, Shokaku, and Zuikaku launched a first wave of 183 aircraft: torpedo bombers, dive bombers, high-level bombers, and Zero fighters, under the operational command of Commander Mitsuo Fuchida. A US Army radar station at Opana on the northern tip of Oahu picked up the incoming formation shortly after 07:00 but was told the blip was probably a group of American B-17 bombers due in from California. A miniature Japanese submarine was spotted and sunk off the harbour entrance by the destroyer USS Ward at 06:37; the report was in bureaucratic transit when the attack began.

At about 07:48 local time Fuchida broadcast the codeword 'To, To, To' and then, taking the American defenders by complete surprise, 'Tora, Tora, Tora' back to the fleet, indicating that surprise had been achieved. Torpedo bombers came in from the north-west and released shallow-running torpedoes fitted with wooden stabiliser fins against the eight battleships moored in two rows along 'Battleship Row' on Ford Island. Dive bombers hit the airfields at Wheeler, Hickam, Ewa, and Ford Island, catching most American aircraft parked in tight anti-sabotage rows and destroying them on the ground. The battleship USS Arizona was struck by a large armour-piercing bomb that detonated its forward magazine at 08:06, killing 1,177 crew almost instantly; her burning hulk settled on the harbour bottom. The USS Oklahoma capsized after multiple torpedo hits, trapping some 429 sailors. The USS California, West Virginia, Nevada, and Utah were also sunk or grounded, with the Maryland, Tennessee, and Pennsylvania badly damaged.

A second wave of 171 aircraft arrived around 08:50 and continued the attack against ships and airfields until roughly 09:45. By the time the last Japanese aircraft returned to their carriers, some 2,403 Americans were dead, 1,178 wounded, and the Pacific battle line was on the harbour floor. Japanese losses were 29 aircraft and five midget submarines, with 64 servicemen killed. Nagumo, worried about American submarines and the whereabouts of the three American aircraft carriers (Enterprise, Lexington, and Saratoga were all at sea and thus escaped), declined to launch a third strike against Pearl Harbor's fuel tanks, dockyards, and submarine base, a decision later much criticised. The declaration of war that Japanese diplomats were meant to deliver in Washington before the attack was, through decoding delays at the embassy, handed over more than an hour after the bombs had fallen.

image.caption: The USS Arizona burning after the detonation of her forward magazine, 7 December 1941.
image.credit: US Navy, via National Archives and Records Administration and Wikimedia Commons

sections.heading: Aftermath & consequences
sections.body:
On 8 December 1941 President Roosevelt addressed a joint session of Congress and asked for a declaration of war against Japan, calling 7 December 'a date which will live in infamy'. Congress voted for war within an hour, with only Representative Jeannette Rankin of Montana in dissent. Britain, whose Malayan and Hong Kong territories were already under Japanese attack, declared war on Japan the same day. On 11 December Adolf Hitler, honouring the Tripartite Pact but under no strict legal obligation, declared war on the United States; Congress reciprocated within hours. The European and Asian wars were now one global conflict.

Japan's Southeast Asian offensive rolled forward with astonishing speed. Guam fell on 10 December; Wake Island after a bitter defence on 23 December; Hong Kong on Christmas Day; Manila on 2 January 1942; Singapore, in the largest capitulation in British military history, on 15 February 1942; the Dutch East Indies by March; and Bataan and Corregidor in the Philippines by early May. Yet the American carriers survived, and the first strategic checks came quickly: the Doolittle Raid on Tokyo in April 1942, the drawn Battle of the Coral Sea in May, and the crushing American victory at Midway on 4-7 June 1942, which sank four of the six Pearl Harbor carriers. Yamamoto himself was killed in April 1943 when his aircraft was shot down over Bougainville by American fighters guided by intercepted radio traffic.

At home the war reshaped the United States. Roughly sixteen million Americans served in uniform; women moved into war industries in unprecedented numbers; and gross national product roughly doubled between 1939 and 1945. It also produced Executive Order 9066 of 19 February 1942, under which some 120,000 people of Japanese ancestry, most of them American citizens, were interned in camps in the western interior, a policy for which Congress formally apologised and paid reparations in 1988. In the Pacific, the American 'island-hopping' campaign, coupled with a devastating submarine offensive against Japanese merchant shipping and firebombing of Japanese cities in 1944-45, drove the war back to the home islands and ended with the atomic bombings and Japanese surrender in August 1945.

image.caption: President Roosevelt signs the declaration of war against Japan, 8 December 1941.
image.credit: US National Archives, via Wikimedia Commons

sections.heading: Legacy
sections.body:
Pearl Harbor became the founding trauma of American postwar strategic thinking. The commanders on the spot, Admiral Husband E. Kimmel and Lieutenant General Walter Short, were relieved within days; nine commissions of inquiry between 1941 and 1946 examined the intelligence failures. The lesson taken away, that a strategic surprise attack could reach the American heartland, was written into the founding statutes of the Central Intelligence Agency (1947), the National Security Council, the joint intelligence system, and the postwar practice of forward-deployed naval and air forces around the Eurasian rim.

The attack has attracted a durable revisionist literature arguing that Roosevelt knew of the attack in advance and allowed it to happen to secure American entry into the war. Serious historians have consistently rejected this thesis: the balance of evidence is that Washington expected an attack somewhere in Southeast Asia and misread the strategic warning signals about Hawaii. Debate continues, however, about how much clearer the warning could have been made if intelligence had been better coordinated, and about the human cost of not launching an American third strike or of the mid-air detonation on USS Arizona.

Memorial culture has been unusually intense. The USS Arizona Memorial, opened in 1962 and administered by the National Park Service, sits atop the sunken battleship, whose hulk continues to seep small quantities of fuel oil more than eighty years after the attack. It is one of the most visited historic sites in the United States. In Japan the attack occupies a more ambiguous place: a moment of tactical brilliance leading, as Yamamoto had feared, to national catastrophe. The date is remembered above all as the moment the United States entered the Second World War and, in ways felt everywhere from Berlin to Beijing, took its place at the centre of the twentieth-century world order.

worldContext.text: The European war in December 1941 was at a hinge. Nazi Germany's invasion of the Soviet Union, launched on 22 June, had reached the outskirts of Moscow but was halted by the Red Army's counter-offensive under General Georgy Zhukov beginning on 5-6 December, in temperatures below minus thirty. The siege of Leningrad had begun. In North Africa Rommel's Afrika Korps and British Commonwealth forces were locked in Operation Crusader around Tobruk. Occupied Europe endured its second winter of Nazi rule; the mass shootings of Soviet Jews had begun and the Wannsee Conference on the 'Final Solution' was five weeks away.

British India was already fully mobilised for the war, with more than a million volunteers under arms in the Middle East, East Africa, and Malaya. Congress leaders, imprisoned during the individual satyagraha of 1940-41, had been released in December 1941. Within weeks of Pearl Harbor, Japanese forces would drive the British out of Burma and reach the eastern gates of India. Sir Stafford Cripps's mission of March-April 1942, offering dominion status after the war in exchange for wartime cooperation, would be rejected and Gandhi would launch the Quit India movement in August.

British forces under General Claude Auchinleck were engaged with Erwin Rommel's Panzer Army Africa in the Libyan desert; Tobruk, besieged since April 1941, had just been relieved in the drawn-out Operation Crusader. In the Levant, British and Free French forces had ejected the Vichy French from Syria and Lebanon in July 1941 and toppled the pro-Axis Rashid Ali government in Iraq. In Iran, Anglo-Soviet forces had overthrown Reza Shah in August-September 1941, opened a Persian Corridor for lend-lease supplies to the Soviet Union, and installed his son Mohammad Reza Pahlavi.

Africa in December 1941 was a continent at war on multiple fronts. Italian East Africa had fallen to Commonwealth forces, and Ethiopia had been restored to Haile Selassie in May 1941. Vichy French administrations still held much of West and Equatorial Africa; Free French forces under Charles de Gaulle and Felix Eboue controlled Chad, Cameroon, and French Equatorial Africa and were fighting alongside the British in Libya. In South Africa, Prime Minister Jan Smuts's government pushed hard on war production and recruited African labour battalions.

Latin America mostly followed the American lead into war. Within days of Pearl Harbor, most Central American and Caribbean republics declared war on the Axis; Mexico and Brazil would follow later after German submarine attacks on their shipping. Canada, already at war since 1939, sent troops to reinforce the losing garrison of Hong Kong. In the United States, the industrial mobilisation directed by the War Production Board and unions such as the CIO transformed the labour market and, over four years, doubled national output while raising real wages.

Australia and New Zealand were suddenly on the front line. Within hours of the Pearl Harbor attack Japanese forces landed in Malaya and Thailand; by early 1942 they would take Rabaul, bomb Darwin (19 February 1942), and threaten Port Moresby. Australian prime minister John Curtin issued a much-quoted New Year message that Australia looked to America 'free of any pangs as to our traditional links or kinship with the United Kingdom'; the ANZUS relationship began to take shape in this crisis. New Zealand recalled part of its 2nd Division from Egypt to defend the Pacific.

figures.name: Franklin D. Roosevelt
figures.role: President of the United States, delivered the 'day of infamy' address to Congress on 8 December 1941
figures.credit: Leon Perskie, FDR Presidential Library, via Wikimedia Commons

figures.name: Isoroku Yamamoto
figures.role: Commander-in-Chief of the Combined Fleet of the Imperial Japanese Navy, architect of the Pearl Harbor plan
figures.credit: Imperial Japanese Navy, via Wikimedia Commons

figures.name: Chuichi Nagumo
figures.role: Vice Admiral commanding the First Air Fleet (Kido Butai) that struck Pearl Harbor
figures.credit: Imperial Japanese Navy, via Wikimedia Commons

figures.name: Mitsuo Fuchida
figures.role: Commander of the air groups on the mission, sent the 'Tora, Tora, Tora' signal
figures.credit: Imperial Japanese Navy, via Wikimedia Commons

figures.name: Husband E. Kimmel
figures.role: Commander-in-Chief of the US Pacific Fleet at Pearl Harbor, relieved of command after the attack

figures.name: Hideki Tojo
figures.role: Prime minister of Japan from October 1941, presided over the decision for war
figures.credit: Government of Japan, via Wikimedia Commons

images.caption: The USS Arizona burning after the detonation of her forward magazine during the Japanese attack on Pearl Harbor, 7 December 1941.
images.credit: US Navy, via National Archives and Records Administration and Wikimedia Commons
